speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. And this is tape number 11. And now we shall speak about the evacuation of Auschwitz, about your leaving Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it-- when did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happpend on the 18th of January '45. It started in the morning. We saw all this kind of marching and people were cold, and we had to take one blanket. And we went out before the gate, and they were distributing for each one, one or two loafs of bread and that was all. And they were counting us endlessly, until it was dark. And I remember when it started to get dark, we were marching out. And I remember even time-- short time before this started, to demolished the crematorium. I remember the crematorium running too. Then crematorium was anyhow demolished partly from before. But more and more demolition. And just when we are leaving, we could hear how they put-- must have been dynamite or what--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans bombed--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they demolished the crematorium [INAUDIBLE]. And I remember also that there was tremendous chaos at the evacuation, and that enabled these few-- I imagine there were about 20 or so-- Sonderkommando people to somehow enter our line. And the SS were looking for them. Because in this chaos, they just mixed with us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Run away, other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this way, they escaped from Auschwitz with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The idea of the Germans was to kill the Sonderkommados.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. They were the very first in the list to be annihilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: In order that they won't tell what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was anyhow not many left after the uprising. They left only a small group for their immediate need for burning dead corpse. I don't think there were much gassing afterwards. Maybe they were shooting, killing some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of them, about 20, got into your lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20. And I remember I met a few of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two or three I met in Mount Hausen in the beginning. Then I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suffered a lot in [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz. But it was peanuts from the time from the todesmaersche. What we called the todesmaersche, the marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? It was a very, very severe winter. And in the beginning, I think it was three days we had to march, more or less without any rest, day and night. And we march, and we marched. And there was colonies of other people, and all that we could see next to us on both sides people who were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lying around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lying near the [INAUDIBLE] on the [INAUDIBLE] right and left. And this gray mass of the brain running out from the-- from-- from the skull. And we knew, if we stop, that is our death. And also, if you are in the last row-- because people slowly, slowly couldn't go any further, and were behind. And the ones that were behind were shot. Or the ones who sat down. Also it is winter and terribly tired. And we were half dreaming because we couldn't sleep and we had to go on. Our luck was that we, I mean the children, helped as far as we could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each other we gave a hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of your group were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many-- there were not many left. Because we lost track. But lets say a group of 10 children were more or less in the same colony. And we helped each other as far as we could. But the immediate danger and slowly losing our-- our strengths, and always hearing this shouting around and killing, seeing the dead corpse, and SS around us, [GERMAN]. And so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were SS people who were walking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course they were SS people were walking with us. And we know that is-- that is our end if we stop or if we are left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you are going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We went and it was day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: After three days, we came to Blechhammer, which was a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blechhammer. Blechhammer was one of the out-- in Silesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was it? In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yeah. And all that I remember, as I told you, it was so horrible, that I could say this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way to Blechhmaer. Thank God that my father was gone. He wouldn't be able in his age and in his condition-- he was nearly a Muselmann-- to survive this march, and he would surely be killed like everyone who couldn't go any further. And I thought it's more, a kind of better death, I thought at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what you thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to suffer because I hardly could endure it. And I remember also that next to us [INAUDIBLE] were the children from Lodz, or Litzmannstadt, and later on they told us this story how it was in the Lodz. And they had it-- well, they had it from years, since '39, a very, very bad time. And even in Lodz, they suffered enormously, much more than us physically. I mean, we were left from many, many thousands of children. But at least, comparatively, we had it better. They were starved, and one of the children told me, well, if somebody was very sick, the doctor in Lodz prescribed him 200 grams or something like that of rotten potato peels. That's what they could give him as an extra kind of ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were children that you met on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even before, because they were with us, and they came with us out. So they didn't have so much strength as us. Somehow we arrived in Blechhammer. We had a night, maybe, of rest, and then came the [INAUDIBLE] day, which was Sonderdienst, a special unit of the SS, of German. Sonderdienst, they usually were designed for the worst kind of jobs, and in a very cruel way, they again--

speaker: interviewer
text: Set you off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took us for a march, and we came to a railway station, and we were put into wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: But some were closed and some were open. And our luck that we children had it again a little better because we came in this very severe winter in a closed cattle wagon, which again meant better chance for surviving. The condition there were really horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the waggon.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, we were lucky that we were between us, only--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people? How many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say, but there were about 80 or 100 in one wagon. There was two or three wagons like that, but there were some open wagons.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this cold

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew afterwards that the ones that were in open wagons were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Frozen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of them were frozen to death. In some wagon, where Russian former POV, or older prisoners--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --They squeezed-- non Jews-- the Jews, let's say they squeezed all of the Jews into half of the waggon and they took the rest. Which again meant for the people who were left, this horrible condition, death. And so died my friend [NON-ENGLISH], I told you about. He was in such a cattle wagon with other prisoners, older men, who took advantage that they were stronger and they took from the children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Their place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their place, which meant death. We didn't get at the beginning any food, any water. And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train. We found some empty conserves, and some kind of drinks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow we had it with us, and we somehow threw it out from the wagon and could collect a bit of snow, which we melted, and that was our water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, on the way, it was again of luck, I remember, near Katowice, suddenly there was some possibly English airplanes, bombed a train of German soldiers who were next to us. And some of the soldiers went under our trains because they hoped this way they would be safe. But by machine guns they killed them, and of course many of us. Again, what meant luck-- in these wagons there was a tiny little window and everyone tried to be near this window to have a bit of fresh air, and we took turns. I remember just I had my turn, but I gave it to somebody else, to a friend of mine, and exactly where he stood, he was shot to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bullet.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bullet. And somebody else from our group was also wounded or killed. Anyhow, in the next station they took the dead people out and just throw them out. I don't know what happened. We passed again, because it was a very well known railway connection in the station, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, which now was the second time, or third time I passed it. When I went to Theresienstadt, from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz, and now from Auschwitz to-- on the way to I didn't know where. But anyhow, we stopped there. It was the early morning. People, workers, went to work. And they saw us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were on a little bridge like on the railway station, and they had their lunch and they throw it to us. And especially I could somehow to communicate with them, because they couldn't guard the whole train.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you spoke the language, you spoke Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke the language and therefore, into our wagon they brought us a bit of coffee. Their own--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans didn't object?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they couldn't have their eyes everywhere, and because it was mixed with civil population, somehow we got some food into our wagon, which surely saved us, because the journey continued quite a long time. I remember we passed Prague. We were somewhere in a railway station near Prague. We went further on. On the way, one SS let us once out to make toilet. Before, what we could do is when-- I mean, I told you we had this melted snow, and we took our blankets and we made little square pieces like a handkerchief and as long as there was something left of the blankets, we went on the toilet on these blankets and we throw it out. But later, even this we couldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no buckets there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. But that was that. After quite a long time, it was about I guess a week or maybe longer, we arrived at Mauthausen. I didn't know that it's Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we arrived in Austria, and I still mostly had some aesthetical something in me and I looked. It was completely different from Auschwitz, because there were mountains and the buildings were from stones. You know in Mauthausen, they have quarries. And everything was made from stone. It was completely different. In the looks from Birkenau--

speaker: interviewer
text: Birkenau especially.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the surrounding was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: The area.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a sunny day and blue skies. But when we arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was January.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the end of January.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess around the 25 of January, something like that. But we had to walk quite a while. Many couldn't do it. And we saw again the prisoners and we came to Mauthausen. And there, there was some very, for us, old prisoner, meaning there many years. Some were even 12 years since '39, political prisoners and so on. And I remember one was even a Czech kind of kapo. He was a good man. But his nerves were in such a stage. I've never seen somebody so nervous. A bundle of nerves. But he didn't beat us. So he behaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in the beginning in barracks, and slowly they divided us into little groups. And they older ones had to do all kinds of works. But I remember the conditions were horrible. Because we slept somehow on the floor. Slept is understatement. Like sardines. I mean my head on foot of somebody else. So always head and foot, head and foot. And they were squeezing us really like sardines. The trouble was if somebody wanted to get out at night, he couldn't more squeeze himself into the last position. Then there was a shortage of water and of food. We had less and less food. But I do remember, we came to again a closed block. The blockeldester was [INAUDIBLE] against [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] was a kind of the second after the blockeldester. Both were German criminals. Sadists like that. I didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sadists.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so before. They behaved in the most outrageous cruel way. They [INAUDIBLE] us and--

speaker: interviewer
text: They what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always they wanted to clean the block, so it doesn't matter if it was snow or terrible weather, we had to go out and some of them had to clean the floor in this block. We meanwhile could die of cold and this terrible winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were children together?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children and grown ups, partisans from Yugoslavia, I remember. And he was behaving in most cruel way. And I remember his speech, when he said-- because at that time, they knew the war is going slowly to the end-- when the war-- when the German-- oh, sorry-- when the Russian will be five kilometers from here, I will take my knife, he would say, [GERMAN], he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hairdresser.

speaker: interviewee
text: A hairdresser. And all of you, I will kill you until your Jewish blood will be sprinkling here on the wall. And terrible speeches.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the blockeldester?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the second. But also the block was--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: German criminals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know only [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know his second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second name, no. I knew it-- and I own knew that he was lynched after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have been in this block. But even in this block-- of course, what happened when we came, we went again through [INAUDIBLE]. They took everything we had. I remember I was very, very clever, and even from Auschwitz, I managed to smuggle a box with cigarettes, some 20 cigarettes. And that was in my shoes. How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was barely 15. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you smoking, yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But I smuggled the cigarettes and I somehow was able to bring some to Mauthausen. And AT Mauthausen I managed to change it for a special, I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three rations of soup. And in Mauthausen, there were many ration, and POVs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were treated in the most cruel way. Sometimes the German led them naked and pour on them cold-- I mean water they were frozen to death and so on. I remember there was a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the temperature in Mauthausen at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely, 10, 15 under. And I remember, there were Czech, non Jews, prisoners, political prisoners, who behaved most wonderfully to us, and they let us have their ration of soup. And it was whole procedure how they've done it. They were living in different compounds of course, and they left the soup under the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were children.

speaker: interviewee
text: You must imagine, only in a block which was more or less in better condition, somebody left his ration of soup--

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone else--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it would be impossible to do it in Auschwitz or in late in Birkenau or in the Mauthausen between us. But the old prisoners had their own blocks where their situation was much better. So he told us I go to work but I leave you children some soup. And it was a whole job to get out of our block of our courtyard, but we managed. And we took the soup. And we brought it to our camp. Once it was me. And I remember the greatest temptation in my life was that I had now this soup and I had to share it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the whole pot?

speaker: interviewee
text: A pot-- I don't know, a little soup. But at that time, it was like gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Treasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew everyone had four spoons let's say, of soup. We could divide it between our little group of children. I had my four spoons. I was able to finish the whole soup. I was able to eat five spoons and it was the greatest temptation in my life not to do it and to share it. Because it's very difficult to imagine what it is if you're in such a situation and have food to share it with somebody else. But we still had this kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Conditions were worse than in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much worse. I mean, people were dying of hunger. And even the beginning wasn't so bad to say months or two months later. I speak now about the end of--

speaker: interviewer
text: January,

speaker: interviewee
text: --January, beginning of February. We had a sadist blockeldester. He took our rations. Put it in a blanket, trampled on it. So they were just crumbs. And sometimes the crumbs were [NON-ENGLISH], how you call it, [NON-ENGLISH] with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, they were not edible. You couldn't eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we couldn't eat it, but then what he gave us and even these few crumbs, they were tasteless, and we had to sell a few of these crumbs to get a few drops of salt, et cetera. But he beat up people in the most-- the shouting of the poor Jews who were beaten by him, it's unforgettable. He was really a sadist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Mauthausen. But that was only the beginning. Later on, it was worse and worse, because there was evacuation of all camps from the east, and they were brought to the west. All the former concentration camps were over-- overfilled with prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're speaking of Mauthausen. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm speaking of Mauthausen. There came more and more transports. And then there came transports from Hungarian Jews and women. And this was the so-called Munka Tabor. Munka Tabor is [INAUDIBLE] Hungarian working camps. And they came also by Todesmarsche and so on or by train to Mauthausen. And Mauthausen was overfilled. Some people went further on to [INAUDIBLE] and other camps. And they had to work in these under--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in mines. And this factory where they have-- where they produce parts of the firearms, the rockets, in horrible condition under the ground. We, who were a little group of children, were lucky we were staying in Mauthausen. At least not all of us went to a very severe work. Sometimes we didn't went at all to work. But slowly, we were losing weight and energy and even then we were transported to another camp in my Mauthausen, the so-called Zelt. Zelt means--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: A huge tent. And these conditions there were indescribable. Really without food, without anything. Head full of lices. I remember I couldn't get up when I was lying in the mud or whatever. I did try to just to sit down, I was just white for my eyes. We were slowly dying, and around people were dying. Then I remember one date it was bombardment by the Americans and our [GERMAN] was near a SS camp. And the bomb fell seconds before and fell into our camp. And afterwards, there were cases which I saw of cannibalism.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, there were dead bodies all around, and the prisoners who were a little stronger took hold of them and one who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Took hold of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the pieces of the bodies. And I saw it as some of them tried even to boil it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say because I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do actually in cannibalism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took off pieces of meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieces of meat, as food and they ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they cook it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They cooked it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones who had the possibility, they cooked it. One of a very young prisoner who told me proudly-- was very primitive and there were many in the camps-- and he tod me, oh, look how lucky I was. There was just some bodies in the [GERMAN], which nobody got it, and I was one of the first. And I could take off a bit from the hips, which means there was still not only bones, but a little flesh left. That was his great luck. So people were starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw exactly this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually you saw it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not only me there were many who saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that you were told that this happened, you saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw it with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does it look?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see something which is looking like some pieces of meat. And there were many other prisoners who tried to find some food. And we children tried to find some-- some-- something which was still growing, grass. But even there was no grass. It was a severe winter. Sometimes we found the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- how you call it? Off the grass, which is inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: The part that is inside the earth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The part that is inside the earth. Sometimes we were lucky. We found something we could eat. The hunger was undescribable. I remember people who came from Hungary and had some golden rings, which the Germany and his great cowardice, didn't succeed yet to take away, took their golden rings and gave it just for two spoons of soup. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. Mr. Bakon, we should stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we should just go on in a second.
